parts.title: Part 1 – Everyday Social Context
parts.description: A conversation between two people in a social or semi-official context.
parts.transcript:
Good morning, City Sports Centre. How can I help you?
Hi, yes, I'd like to enquire about joining the centre and getting some information about your membership options.
Of course! We have a few options. The Basic membership is £25 per month and gives you access to the gym and swimming pool. Our Premium membership is £45 per month and includes group fitness classes, sauna, and a free personal trainer session each month.
That sounds great. I'm particularly interested in the swimming pool. What are the pool opening hours?
The pool is open Monday to Friday from 6am until 10pm, and on weekends from 7am to 8pm. We also have lane swimming sessions reserved for serious swimmers every Tuesday and Thursday morning from 6 to 7:30.
Wonderful. And what about parking? Is there parking available?
Yes, we have a car park on Bolton Road, just behind the centre. It holds about 150 vehicles. Members get free parking, but you do need to register your vehicle number plate at reception.
Perfect. One more thing — I have a 12-year-old daughter. Are there any junior programmes?
Absolutely. We run a Junior Swim Academy on Saturdays at 9am and 11am, divided by ability. We also have a junior gym programme for ages 14 and up with qualified supervision. For your daughter's age, the swim academy would be the right fit. It's an additional £30 per term on top of a junior membership.
Great. Could I book a tour of the facility before I sign up?
Of course. Tours run every weekday at 10am and 2pm. You can book online or just pop in. May I take your name and a contact number?
Yes, it's Sarah — Sarah Collins. And my number is 07821 446 390.
Lovely. And your email?
It's s.collins@mailhub.co.uk.
Thank you Sarah. We'll confirm your tour booking by email. Is there anything else?
No, that's everything. Thank you very much.
You're welcome. Goodbye!

questions.text: Basic membership monthly cost: £___
Premium membership monthly cost: £___
What does the Premium membership NOT include?
Pool weekend closing time: ___pm
Car park location: ___ Road
Car park capacity: approximately ___ vehicles
The Junior Swim Academy is suitable for the caller's daughter because:
options: C. She is an experienced swimmer

questions.text: Junior Swim Academy additional cost per term: £___
Caller's surname: ___
Caller's email domain: ___@mailhub.co.uk

parts.title: Part 2 – Public Information
parts.description: A monologue in a social context, such as a speech or announcement.
parts.transcript:
Welcome to the Greenfield Community Library's podcast series. I'm Tom Wheeler, the head librarian, and today I'll be telling you about some exciting new services and changes we've introduced this season.
First, our opening hours. We are now open Monday through Saturday from 8am until 9pm. On Sundays, we open at 10am and close at 5pm. This is a change from our previous schedule, so please take note.
We've also launched a brand new digital lending service. You can now borrow up to ten eBooks and five audiobooks simultaneously with a valid library card. The loan period is three weeks, and you can renew once online if no one else has reserved the title.
Our children's reading corner has been completely renovated. We've installed new interactive reading pods with built-in tablets, and we run story time sessions every Wednesday at 4pm and Saturday at 11am, aimed at children aged 3 to 8.
For adults, we've partnered with the National Literacy Trust to offer free weekly workshops on digital skills, creative writing, and financial literacy. These take place every Thursday evening at 6:30pm and spaces must be booked in advance through our website or at the reception desk.
We've added a new quiet study zone on the second floor. This area has 24 individual desks with power sockets and a no-phone policy. Access requires your library card. Group study rooms on the ground floor can now be booked online for up to three hours per session.
Finally, our summer reading challenge kicks off on the 15th of July. Children who complete six books over the summer will receive a certificate and a small prize. Registration forms are available at the children's desk.
Please visit our website at greenfield-library.org or call us on 01452 887 300 for more details. Thank you.

questions.text: New Saturday closing time: ___pm
Maximum eBooks borrowable simultaneously: ___
Digital loan period: ___ weeks
Story time sessions target age group is:
Adult workshops take place every ___ evening
Workshop start time: ___pm
The quiet study zone is located on the:
Maximum group study room booking per session: ___ hours
Summer reading challenge start date: 15th of ___
Library phone number: 01452 ___ 300

parts.title: Part 3 – Academic Discussion
parts.description: A conversation between two or more speakers in an educational context.
parts.transcript:
Professor Evans: Come in, Daniel. Sit down. So, how are you getting on with your research proposal for the environmental policy module?
Daniel: It's coming along, but I'm having trouble narrowing down my focus. I originally wanted to write about renewable energy policy in the UK, but I think the scope might be too broad.
Professor Evans: You're right to identify that. A common mistake students make is trying to cover too much ground. What specific aspect interests you most?
Daniel: I'm most interested in the barriers to offshore wind energy adoption — particularly the economic and regulatory challenges.
Professor Evans: That's a much tighter focus, and very relevant given recent government targets. Have you started your literature review?
Daniel: I've read several journal articles from the past five years. I found a 2021 paper by Thornton and James quite useful — it outlines the main funding gaps in offshore wind projects.
Professor Evans: Good choice. You should also look at the work of Dr. Patel from the University of Cambridge. Her research on grid infrastructure bottlenecks is highly relevant to what you're exploring.
Daniel: I'll search for that. My plan is to use a mixed-methods approach — quantitative analysis of investment data alongside qualitative interviews with industry experts.
Professor Evans: Interesting. However, I'd caution you on the interviews. Getting access to industry professionals for academic research can be time-consuming and may delay your submission. Have you considered using publicly available parliamentary reports as a proxy?
Daniel: I hadn't thought of that. That's a really practical suggestion.
Professor Evans: It would still give you rich qualitative data without the access problems. Now, how many words is your proposal?
Daniel: The limit is 5,000 words and I'm currently at about 2,200.
Professor Evans: You have room to develop your methodology section more. Also ensure you address limitations — that's where many proposals fall short. Have you considered ethical considerations?
Daniel: Since I'm not interviewing humans directly, I thought ethics clearance wasn't needed.
Professor Evans: Double check that with the department office. Data sourcing — even secondary data — can have ethical dimensions, especially around proprietary information. Submit your revised draft to me by the 20th, and we'll meet again the following week.

questions.text: Daniel's original research topic was too:
Daniel's narrowed focus: barriers to ___ wind energy adoption
The Thornton and James (2021) paper focuses on:
Dr. Patel works at the University of ___
The professor's concern about interviews is:
Suggested alternative to interviews: parliamentary ___
Word limit for the proposal: ___ words
Daniel's current word count: approximately ___
The professor says proposals often fall short on:
Revised draft submission deadline: the ___th

parts.title: Part 4 – Academic Lecture
parts.description: A monologue on an academic subject.
parts.transcript:
Good morning everyone. Today I'd like to talk about a phenomenon that has fundamentally changed human cognition — or at least, that's what some researchers are claiming. I'm referring to what psychologist Linda Henkel at Fairfield University calls the "photo-taking impairment effect."
The premise is straightforward. When we photograph an object or experience, we may actually remember it less well than if we had simply observed it. The reason, according to Henkel, is that taking a photo allows the mind to "offload" the memory to the device, reducing the cognitive effort invested in encoding the experience.
In a series of studies conducted at a museum, participants were divided into two groups. One group photographed the artefacts they were shown; the other merely observed them. When tested the following day, the photographing group recalled significantly fewer details and showed weaker object recognition than the observation group.
However, this isn't the full story. Follow-up research introduced a third condition — zoomed photography, where participants used a camera's zoom function to focus closely on a specific part of an object. Remarkably, this group performed as well as the observation-only group, suggesting that active visual engagement — looking closely through the lens — compensates for the cognitive offloading effect.
This has implications beyond personal photography. Consider the rise of social media documentation culture. When people attend concerts, lectures, or sporting events, many spend substantial time recording content for digital sharing. Researchers like Dr. Alixandra Barasch at NYU have studied this behaviour and found that the motivation behind the recording matters.
If people record for personal memory purposes, the impairment effect tends to be stronger. But if the intent is to share with others — a more social motivation — the engagement with the experience remains relatively high, and memory suffers less. The social purpose seems to keep the brain more actively involved.
What should we take from this? Not that we should stop photographing our experiences. But understanding when and how we capture moments can help us engage more deeply. The evidence suggests that conscious, focused photography — not passive, reflexive snapping — may actually complement rather than undermine our experiential memory.

questions.text: The 'photo-taking impairment effect' was coined by: Linda ___
According to Henkel, photos impair memory because:
options: B. They offload memory to a device
C. They take too long to take

questions.text: The museum study tested participants:
The third condition involved ___ photography
The zoomed photography group performed:
Dr. Barasch works at ___ University
Recording for personal memory causes:
Recording for social sharing results in:
options: D. The same as personal recording

questions.text: The lecture's main conclusion is that:
options: A. We should stop taking photos
C. Conscious photography can complement memory

questions.text: The 'cognitive offloading effect' refers to:
options: A. Physical tiredness from holding a camera
B. The brain reducing effort when a device stores memory
C. Forgetting to charge your device

passages.title: Passage 1 – The Science of Sleep
passages.text:
Sleep is one of the most universal and least understood behaviours in the animal kingdom. For decades, scientists have struggled to explain why living organisms devote so much time to a state of apparent inactivity. Today, however, a growing body of research suggests that sleep is far from passive — it is a period of intense biological activity crucial to survival, learning, and emotional wellbeing.

The most accepted theory of sleep function is the restorative hypothesis. According to this view, sleep allows the body and brain to repair themselves. During slow-wave sleep — the deepest phase — the pituitary gland releases growth hormone, which stimulates tissue repair and muscle growth. The immune system also becomes more active during sleep, producing cytokines that help the body fight infection and inflammation. Studies have consistently shown that sleep-deprived individuals suffer impaired immune responses, increased levels of stress hormones such as cortisol, and accelerated cellular ageing.

A second theory focuses on memory consolidation. Research by neuroscientists including Matthew Walker at UC Berkeley has demonstrated that during REM (Rapid Eye Movement) sleep, the brain replays and strengthens neural pathways associated with experiences from the preceding day. In one well-known experiment, participants who slept after learning a motor task outperformed those who remained awake, suggesting that sleep actively processes and integrates new information.

However, the relationship between sleep and memory is not straightforward. Not all memories are treated equally. Emotional memories, particularly those associated with fear, appear to be preferentially consolidated during REM sleep. Meanwhile, procedural memories — the "how to" knowledge that underlies skills — appear to depend more on slow-wave sleep. This distinction has significant implications for understanding post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), where unwanted emotional memories are repeatedly consolidated and replayed.

Sleep architecture — the pattern and sequencing of sleep stages — also varies considerably across the lifespan. Newborns spend up to 50 percent of their sleep in the REM phase, compared with roughly 20 percent in healthy adults. This disparity has led researchers to propose that REM sleep plays a particularly critical role in early brain development, helping to establish the neural architecture that underpins cognitive function throughout life.

Despite its importance, modern society treats sleep as a luxury. The average adult in industrialised nations now sleeps 6.5 to 7 hours per night — a reduction of more than an hour since the 1940s. This cultural devaluation of sleep has measurable consequences. Epidemiological studies have linked chronic sleep restriction to increased risk of obesity, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and depression. Shift workers, who frequently experience disrupted circadian rhythms, show markedly elevated rates of these conditions.

The science of sleep is still evolving. One of the most intriguing recent discoveries concerns the glymphatic system — a waste-clearance mechanism in the brain that operates almost exclusively during sleep. During waking hours, metabolic byproducts including beta-amyloid proteins — implicated in Alzheimer's disease — accumulate in the spaces between brain cells. During sleep, the glymphatic system flushes these toxins away. Animal studies have shown that just one night of sleep deprivation can cause a significant buildup of beta-amyloid. Whether this mechanism works identically in humans remains under active investigation, but the implications for neurodegeneration research are profound.

questions.text: Sleep is now understood to be a period of significant biological activity.
Growth hormone is released by the hypothalamus during deep sleep.
Sleep deprivation is associated with higher levels of cortisol.
Matthew Walker's research showed that sleep has no effect on motor learning.
Procedural memories are primarily consolidated during REM sleep.
The connection between REM sleep and PTSD has been definitively resolved.
What percentage of sleep is spent in REM phase by healthy adults?
What does the glymphatic system primarily do?
options: C. Remove metabolic waste from the brain

questions.text: Beta-amyloid proteins are implicated in ___ disease.
The average adult in industrialised nations currently sleeps ___ to 7 hours per night.
According to the passage, which group has markedly elevated rates of health conditions?
It is certain that the glymphatic system works identically in both animals and humans.
REM sleep is believed to play a critical role in early brain development.

passages.title: Passage 2 – The Economics of Urban Green Space
passages.text:
As cities around the world grapple with rapid urbanisation and the worsening effects of climate change, the economic case for urban green space has never been stronger. Parks, street trees, green roofs, and urban forests are no longer considered mere aesthetic amenities — they are increasingly recognised as critical infrastructure that delivers measurable financial benefits to municipalities, businesses, and residents.

The most widely cited economic benefit of urban green space is its effect on property values. Multiple studies across different countries have confirmed a consistent premium for properties near parks and green corridors. Research conducted by the University of Washington found that mature street trees can increase adjacent residential property values by up to 10 percent. In London, the Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors reported that properties within 100 metres of a major green space command an average premium of 5 percent. While this premium benefits property owners directly, it also generates increased tax revenues for local governments.

Urban heat islands — the phenomenon whereby cities trap and radiate significantly more heat than surrounding rural areas — represent a growing economic burden. Cooling costs for commercial and residential buildings are one of the fastest-growing components of municipal energy expenditure. Green infrastructure mitigates this effect through evapotranspiration — the process by which plants release moisture into the atmosphere — and through direct shading. A single mature tree has been estimated to provide cooling equivalent to ten standard air conditioning units operating for 20 hours per day. At scale, strategic urban greening can reduce cooling costs across an entire city district by 10 to 30 percent.

Stormwater management is another area where green infrastructure demonstrates clear economic value. Conventional drainage infrastructure — underground pipes, culverts, and treatment facilities — is expensive to install and maintain. Green roofs, permeable pavements, rain gardens, and riparian buffer zones can absorb and filter significant quantities of rainfall, reducing the burden on conventional systems and decreasing the risk of costly flooding events. Philadelphia's Green City, Clean Waters programme, for instance, uses green infrastructure as its primary mechanism for meeting regulatory water quality targets, projecting long-term savings of over 1 billion dollars compared to the equivalent grey infrastructure solution.

The mental and physical health benefits of urban green space translate into economic value through reduced healthcare costs and increased worker productivity. A landmark study published in the Lancet found that individuals living near high-quality green space reported significantly lower levels of stress and anxiety, and had notably lower rates of cardiovascular disease. Modelling by the World Health Organisation suggests that greening interventions in dense urban environments could reduce premature mortality rates by up to 12 percent in some populations. When these health benefits are translated into economic terms using standard quality-adjusted life year (QALY) frameworks, urban parks consistently emerge as high-value public health investments.

Critics of urban greening investment argue that green space comes at an opportunity cost — land that could be used for housing or commercial development. In cities facing acute housing shortages, this tension is real and politically charged. However, proponents counter that the dichotomy is false. Compact mixed-use development models, green corridors woven into the built fabric of cities, and innovative solutions such as rooftop parks and underground green infrastructure demonstrate that green space and density are not mutually exclusive. Moreover, the long-term economic costs of neglecting green infrastructure — including rising healthcare burdens, increased flood damage, and growing urban heat-related mortality — far outweigh the short-term gains from additional development.

questions.text: Urban green spaces are now classified as critical infrastructure by most governments.
Mature street trees can increase nearby property values by up to 10 percent.
Properties within 200 metres of green space command a 5% premium in London.
Evapotranspiration refers to:
options: B. Moisture released into the atmosphere by plants
C. Heat stored in urban surfaces

questions.text: A single mature tree provides cooling equivalent to ___ standard air conditioning units.
Philadelphia's Green City programme aims to save over how much compared to grey infrastructure?
The Lancet study found green space reduces stress and cardiovascular disease rates.
Greening interventions could reduce premature mortality by up to ___ percent.
What is the main argument of critics of urban greening investment?
options: A. Green space is too expensive to maintain
C. Land could be used for housing or development
D. Green roofs are structurally unsafe

questions.text: The article concludes that green space and high-density development cannot coexist.
QALY frameworks are used to assess health investments.
Rooftop parks are mentioned as examples of innovative green solutions.
According to the passage, cooling costs are:
options: B. One of the fastest-growing energy expenditures
C. A minor concern for municipalities

questions.text: What is 'grey infrastructure' in the context of this passage?

passages.title: Passage 3 – The Attention Economy
passages.text:
In 1971, the social scientist Herbert Simon made a prescient observation: in a world of abundant information, the scarce resource is not information itself but human attention. Decades before the smartphone, Simon identified the fundamental challenge of what is now called the attention economy — an economic system in which the primary commodity is human attention, and technology platforms compete relentlessly to capture, hold, and monetise it.

The mechanisms by which digital platforms exploit psychological vulnerabilities to maximise engagement are now well documented. Variable reward schedules — the same principle that makes slot machines addictive — are embedded in social media feeds and notification systems. Every scroll carries the possibility of reward: an amusing video, a flattering comment, an interesting story. The unpredictability of these rewards activates the brain's dopaminergic pathways in much the same way as gambling. Former executives at companies including Facebook, Google, and Twitter have publicly acknowledged that engagement maximisation was a deliberate and primary design goal, with attention-retention metrics guiding product decisions above all else.

The consequences of this design philosophy extend beyond individual experience. At the societal level, the attention economy has been linked to a measurable decline in sustained concentration. A frequently cited Microsoft study from 2015 claimed that the average human attention span had fallen from 12 seconds to 8 seconds since 2000 — less than that of a goldfish. While this specific claim has been widely disputed by cognitive scientists (attention is not a single, measurable unit, and goldfish cognition is more complex than popular mythology suggests), there is genuine scientific consensus that heavy digital media use is associated with increased cognitive fragmentation, reduced ability to engage in deep focus tasks, and higher rates of task-switching behaviour.

The political implications of the attention economy are particularly troubling. Research in computational propaganda has found that emotionally arousing content — particularly content that provokes outrage or anxiety — spreads significantly faster and further on social media platforms than neutral or calming content. This is not a bug but a feature of engagement-optimised algorithms: emotionally resonant content generates more interaction, more sharing, more time on platform. The result is a media environment structurally biased toward conflict, polarisation, and outrage, which has been implicated in the erosion of shared factual reality and the rise of populist political movements in multiple democracies.

The business model underlying the attention economy is advertising. Users are not customers — they are the product. Their attention, preferences, and behavioural data are aggregated and sold to advertisers in a system that is almost entirely invisible to its participants. The sophistication of this system is remarkable: modern programmatic advertising can target individuals based on thousands of data points, including location history, purchase behaviour, browsing habits, relationship status, and even psychological profiles derived from social media activity.

Regulators in many jurisdictions have begun to respond. The European Union's Digital Services Act, enacted in 2022, imposes significant obligations on large platforms to address systemic risks, including harms related to algorithmic amplification of harmful content. Several countries have introduced or proposed age restrictions on social media, acknowledging the particular vulnerability of adolescents to the psychological effects of engagement-maximised platforms. France, for instance, passed legislation in 2023 requiring parental consent for social media use under the age of 15.

The deeper challenge is structural. So long as digital platforms' revenue is derived from advertising, and so long as advertising revenue is proportional to engagement, the incentive to maximise attention capture will persist regardless of individual platform policies. Some researchers and technologists have proposed alternative business models, including subscription-based platforms, user-owned cooperatives, and algorithmic transparency requirements. Whether any of these alternatives can achieve the network scale necessary to displace incumbent platforms remains, as yet, an open question.

questions.text: Herbert Simon's 1971 observation was about the scarcity of:
Variable reward schedules in apps work on the same principle as slot machines.
The Microsoft study's specific claims about attention spans are universally accepted by scientists.
According to the passage, what type of content spreads fastest on social media?
In the attention economy, users are described as customers of the platform.
The EU's ___ Services Act was enacted in 2022.
France requires parental consent for social media under age 15.
Why does the passage say algorithmic bias toward outrage is 'a feature, not a bug'?
options: A. It was an accident of design
B. It generates more engagement which is the goal

questions.text: Subscription-based platforms have already displaced advertising-based models at scale.
What does 'programmatic advertising' involve?
options: C. Targeting individuals based on data profiles

questions.text: Adolescents are acknowledged by some legislators as particularly vulnerable to attention platforms.
The passage suggests the core problem with attention economy reform is:
options: C. The structural link between advertising revenue and engagement

questions.text: The passage concludes that alternative business models will definitely succeed.

tasks.title: Task 1 – Academic Writing
tasks.prompt: The graph below shows the percentage of households with internet access in four countries between 2005 and 2020.
tasks.instruction: Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.
tasks.chartDescription: Line graph showing internet access percentages: Country A rose from 45% (2005) to 92% (2020). Country B rose from 30% to 88%. Country C rose from 15% to 71%. Country D rose from 8% to 55%.

rubric.taskAchievement: Did you describe the main trends and make comparisons?
rubric.coherence: Is your response logically organised with clear progression?
rubric.lexical: Did you use a variety of vocabulary appropriately?
rubric.grammar: Did you use a range of grammatical structures accurately?

tasks.title: Task 2 – Essay
tasks.prompt: Some people believe that universities should focus solely on academic subjects and theoretical knowledge. Others argue that practical skills and vocational training should also be included in university education.
tasks.instruction: Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience. Write at least 250 words.

rubric.taskAchievement: Did you address all parts of the question and give a clear opinion?
rubric.coherence: Is your essay logically structured with clear paragraphing?
rubric.lexical: Did you use sophisticated and varied vocabulary?
rubric.grammar: Did you use complex grammatical structures with few errors?
